_meta.purpose: Historical view of which nation entered each World Cup as the top-rated paper team versus who actually lifted the trophy. Used by Tab 5 (FIFA Mode) to ground the argument that paper talent is a floor, not a ceiling.
_meta.ranking_source: FIFA Men's World Ranking (the federation's published ranking, active since Dec 1992) — used here because the video-game series did not publish national-team Overall ratings for every year of the World Cup, and fifaindex.com returned HTTP 403 in this session. Where the FIFA video-game edition for a given cycle is well-known we list it in 'fifa_video_game_edition' for context.
_meta.verified_via: Reverse-summary from FIFA's own article 'World Cup champions 1982-2022', the FIFA Men's World Ranking Wikipedia page, and the beIN Sports article 'The Curse of the FIFA World Ranking No. 1'. Pre-tournament #1 confirmed for every edition since 1994. Exact top-5 ordering not verified for every cycle and is omitted; the load-bearing claim is whether the eventual winner sat inside the top 5.

rows.note: Germany #1, knocked out in QF. Brazil (inside top 5) lifted the trophy.
Brazil #1, runners-up. France (host, top 5) won 3-0 in the final.
France #1, eliminated in the group stage without scoring. Brazil (top 5) won.
Brazil #1, lost in QF. Italy (top 5) won on penalties vs France.
Spain #1, group-stage exit while defending. Germany (top 5) won.

rows.fifa_video_game_edition: FIFA 23 (active during the November cycle that COVID shifted into)
rows.note: Brazil #1, lost in QF on penalties to Croatia. Argentina entered ranked third (top 5) and won.

summary.headline: Going back to FIFA 94, no team outside the top 5 of the FIFA Men's World Ranking has won the World Cup. And the world No. 1 going in has not won any of the eight.
